bins.name: 230 kV Rebuttable Presumption
bins.desc: NRG argues the seven-factor test alone will generate costly, years-long disputes that defeat the ANOPR's efficiency goals. It proposes a rebuttable presumption that any load interconnection at 230 kV or above is a transmission-level interconnection subject to Commission jurisdiction. This would complement, not replace, the seven-factor test by providing a clear starting point.
quotes: relying solely on the Commission's seven-factor test, as contemplated by the ANOPR, could lead to a lengthy, opaque, litigious, and uncertain process to determine whether an interconnection is at the transmission level that would result in exactly the sort of uncertainty and delay the ANOPR is meant to avoid.
A rebuttable presumption that a proposed load interconnection at 230 kV or above is a large load interconnection to a transmission facility would avoid these pitfalls.

bins.name: Remove 20 MW Size Restriction
bins.desc: NRG contends the 20 MW threshold is borrowed arbitrarily from generator interconnection rules and has no jurisdictional logic when applied to loads. Small loads do not connect at transmission voltage, and the threshold could trap scaling data centers in state procedures even after they exceed the limit.
quotes: The 20 MW size restriction appears to be arbitrary.  This figure appears to have been selected as the threshold for large loads because it is the threshold for large generators subject to the Commission-prescribed large generator interconnection procedures, rather than the small generator interconnection procedures.
there are likely to be data centers and other large loads whose initial size will be below the 20 MW threshold but which will ramp up in size over time.

bins.name: Full Cost Causation for Network Upgrades
bins.desc: NRG supports requiring interconnecting loads to fund 100 percent of network upgrade costs. It criticizes state revenue-guarantee approaches as failing to match guaranteed payments to actual upgrade cost requirements, which can either force existing customers to subsidize large loads or overcharge the loads themselves.
quotes: NRG strongly supports the Secretary's proposed eighth principle for large load interconnection: that "load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through the interconnection studies."

many state-regulated utilities seek a "revenue guarantee" based on embedded cost rates multiplied by the MW-demand of the data center for some length of time.  Sometimes called "minimum commitments," this provision has been advertised as a policy approach that causes data centers to pay their fair share of grid costs, which may suggest alignment with the eighth principle announced in the ANOPR, but this is not an accurate understanding of the details of the "revenue guarantee" or "minimum commitments" provisions which have so far been adopted by state commissions.

the transmission and distribution revenues guaranteed by a large load addition customer could be substantially more or substantially less the revenue requirement of the transmission and distribution investments that would not have been needed but for that customers' large load addition.

bins.name: Grandfather Existing State Commitments
bins.desc: NRG urges the Commission to preserve the rights of loads already committed under state tariffs, offering them the option to continue under existing procedures or switch to the new federal framework. This mirrors the Commission's approach when standardizing generator interconnection procedures.
quotes: the Commission should provide for the grandfathering of large load's existing interconnection agreements or other arrangements and ensure that large loads whose interconnections are currently being studied are not disadvantaged by the transition to a set of Commission-prescribed standard procedures.
large loads should also have the option of completing the process under the existing procedures or under those adopted in compliance with the final rule.

bins.name: Open Seasons Over Queue Position
bins.desc: NRG argues that simply shifting dysfunctional state queues to FERC jurisdiction solves nothing. It proposes the Commission mandate open seasons modeled on gas pipeline practice, allocating interconnection capacity by NPV rather than request timing. This would clear speculative projects and bring the most megawatts online at the lowest overall cost.
quotes: NRG strongly supports the principles set forth in the ANOPR but proposes several discrete modifications to ensure that the final rule successfully implements those principles.

the ANOPR will not have fulfilled its goals and risen to this critical moment if it merely transfers jurisdiction over dysfunctional load queues from state commissions to FERC or drags large loads into the dysfunction of the generator interconnection queues.

A far better approach would be an open season or an auction mechanism that allocates capacity in a manner that maximizes the net present value ("NPV") of bids as understood through an efficient combination of relevant variables (e.g., least cost, amount of incremental demand capable of being served, and speed to market).

the Commission should mandate the use of open seasons to allocate load interconnection capacity.  FERC-regulated interstate natural gas pipelines have long employed "open seasons to gauge demand for prospective expansion capacity . . . ."

bins.name: Cost Causation and Customer Protection
bins.desc: NRG strongly backs assigning 100 percent of network upgrade costs to the interconnecting load and criticizes state minimum-commitment provisions as disconnected from actual cost causation. Open seasons would also achieve direct cost allocation, and any surplus would reduce costs for existing customers.
quotes: NRG strongly supports the principles set forth in the ANOPR but proposes several discrete modifications to ensure that the final rule successfully implements those principles.

NRG strongly supports the Secretary's proposed eighth principle for large load interconnection: that "load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through the interconnection studies."

many state-regulated utilities seek a "revenue guarantee" based on embedded cost rates multiplied by the MW-demand of the data center for some length of time.  Sometimes called "minimum commitments," this provision has been advertised as a policy approach that causes data centers to pay their fair share of grid costs, which may suggest alignment with the eighth principle announced in the ANOPR, but this is not an accurate understanding of the details of the "revenue guarantee" or "minimum commitments" provisions which have so far been adopted by state commissions.

the transmission and distribution revenues guaranteed by a large load addition customer could be substantially more or substantially less the revenue requirement of the transmission and distribution investments that would not have been needed but for that customers' large load addition.
fail[s] to send a price signal for large load additions to locate in locations where the required transmission . . . investments for the large load would be lower.

This open season or auction achieves a direct cost allocation of both interconnection and network upgrade costs, preventing cost shifts to other customers.  Surplus revenue from the open season should be applied against the transmission owner's transmission revenue requirement, reducing costs for all other customers.

bins.name: Location-Efficient Siting Signals
bins.desc: NRG notes that miscalibrated cost allocation fails to signal to large loads where transmission investments would be cheapest, distorting siting decisions and leading to avoidable grid spending.
quotes: fail[s] to send a price signal for large load additions to locate in locations where the required transmission . . . investments for the large load would be lower.

bins.name: NPV Auction Mechanism Design
bins.desc: NRG lays out a detailed open-season framework: transmission providers first offer existing headroom in an initial auction, then hold subsequent auctions for incremental upgrades. Winning bids are selected by NPV calculated across price, term, quantity, and time-to-energize. Rights obtained through the process should be tradeable and subject to use-it-or-lose-it obligations. The Commission should require the first headroom open season by the first quarter of 2027.
quotes: the ANOPR will not have fulfilled its goals and risen to this critical moment if it merely transfers jurisdiction over dysfunctional load queues from state commissions to FERC or drags large loads into the dysfunction of the generator interconnection queues.

A far better approach would be an open season or an auction mechanism that allocates capacity in a manner that maximizes the net present value ("NPV") of bids as understood through an efficient combination of relevant variables (e.g., least cost, amount of incremental demand capable of being served, and speed to market).

the Commission should mandate the use of open seasons to allocate load interconnection capacity.  FERC-regulated interstate natural gas pipelines have long employed "open seasons to gauge demand for prospective expansion capacity . . . ."

This open season or auction achieves a direct cost allocation of both interconnection and network upgrade costs, preventing cost shifts to other customers.  Surplus revenue from the open season should be applied against the transmission owner's transmission revenue requirement, reducing costs for all other customers.

bins.name: Transition Rights for State Commitments
bins.desc: NRG calls for explicit grandfathering of loads already signed up under state tariffs, with a dual-path option mirroring the generator interconnection transition. Transmission providers should be required to preserve implied queue positions for pending interconnections.
quotes: the Commission should provide for the grandfathering of large load's existing interconnection agreements or other arrangements and ensure that large loads whose interconnections are currently being studied are not disadvantaged by the transition to a set of Commission-prescribed standard procedures.
large loads should also have the option of completing the process under the existing procedures or under those adopted in compliance with the final rule.
